Battle of Colachel

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Battle of Colachel

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Aug 10, 2023

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!IVSh!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5ed27499-88bf-49c4-805f-a89cad54648d_220x298.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!IVSh!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5ed27499-88bf-49c4-805f-a89cad54648d_220x298.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Born in 1706 amidst the flickering oil lamps of Kilimanoor Palace, Marthanda Varma inherited not just royal blood, but a turbulent legacy. The early 18th century in Kerala was a mosaic of fragmented principalities, feuding nobles, and fading colonial ambitions. The Dutch East India Company loomed large, while internal strife threatened to fracture the soul of Travancore.

Thirappur Swaroopam, though modest in stature, became the crucible of transformation. Marthanda Varma, guided by his mother Karthika Thirunal’s political acumen and the martial traditions of Kilimanoor, began consolidating power with ruthless efficiency.

By the early 18th century, the once-proud throne of Venad had become a ceremonial relic. The king was a figurehead, his authority eroded by a triad of forces:

Ettuveetil Pillamar — the landlords of eight powerful houses — wielded real control, their private militias and wealth making them untouchable.

Madampimar, the barons, acted as kingmakers and king-breakers, manipulating succession and policy to serve their estates.

The Yogakkara, a council of eight elders, claimed spiritual and administrative oversight, further diluting royal command.

Together, they formed a shadow government — a decentralized oligarchy that paralyzed governance and stifled reform.

The Dutch East India Company, hungry for pepper and cardamom, enforced a ruthless blockade of the Malabar Coast, choking local trade and asserting naval dominance. Forts dotted the shoreline, not as symbols of protection, but as instruments of extraction.Local rulers were coerced into unfair treaties, their ports turned into Dutch outposts.

The royal lineages of Venad — fragmented and feuding — plunged the kingdom into a cycle of succession disputes and palace intrigues.Assassinations, betrayals, and shifting alliances became the norm, leaving the common people caught in a storm of uncertainty.

When Marthanda Varma ascended the throne in 1729, he did not inherit a kingdom — he seized it from the jaws of chaos. His coronation was not a ritual, but a declaration of war against the entrenched powers that had hollowed out Venad’s sovereignty.

The Ettuveetil Pillamar, once untouchable, were systematically dismantled — their estates confiscated, their militias disbanded, and their influence erased.

The Nair aristocracy, long accustomed to wielding hereditary privilege, found themselves subordinated to the crown.

The Yogakkara, whose spiritual authority had become a political weapon, were stripped of their administrative role.

This was not mere vengeance — it was statecraft with surgical precision, a redefinition of power from clan-based oligarchy to centralized monarchy.

With internal resistance quelled, Marthanda Varma turned his gaze outward — toward the fertile, spice-rich lands that had long been the prize of European powers.

Kollam, a vital port and pepper hub, was annexed, bringing maritime trade under Travancore’s control. Attingal, his maternal inheritance, was absorbed into the crown, symbolizing the fusion of lineage and legitimacy.

Venad, fragmented and feuding, was unified under his iron will.He pushed toward Kochi, challenging Dutch influence and asserting Travancore’s dominance over the spice-producing heartlands.

This was not just territorial expansion — it was economic liberation, reclaiming Kerala’s spice wealth from foreign monopolies and feudal middlemen.

As Marthanda Varma’s banner rose over the spice-rich coast, the Dutch East India Company — once the unchallenged maritime overlord of the Indian Ocean — began to panic. Their monopoly on pepper, cardamom, and cinnamon was slipping through their fingers, not to pirates or rival Europeans, but to a native king who refused to bow.

Gustaaf Van Imhoff, the Dutch Governor, dispatched a stern missive to Marthanda Varma, urging him to halt his conquests.The tone was imperial, the intent unmistakable: a veiled threat cloaked in diplomacy.But Varma, ever the strategist, replied with quiet defiance — his sword spoke louder than any parchment.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vqXz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F42693c7d-85b8-40db-b177-98a43947f745_220x287.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!vqXz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F42693c7d-85b8-40db-b177-98a43947f745_220x287.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Dutch-backed minor kingdoms, fearing absorption into Travancore, joined the fray, hoping to preserve their autonomy. The Dutch deployed naval blockades, fortified coastal outposts, and attempted to rally local resistance. But Marthanda Varma’s forces, trained in modern warfare and loyal to a centralized command, proved superior.

In January 1739, the Dutch Governor of Ceylon, Gustaaf Willem van Imhoff, arrived in Kochi with a mission, to contain Travancore’s expansion before it consumed the Dutch spice empire.

Van Imhoff, alarmed by Marthanda Varma’s annexations, recommended military intervention to protect Dutch commercial interests. He orchestrated a coalition of threatened kingdoms — Kochi, Kollam, Kayamkulam — hoping to encircle Travancore.The Dutch envisioned a swift campaign, backed by naval superiority and local allies.

Van Imhoff met Marthanda Varma in person, expecting to intimidate the king into submission.He offered peace — but with conditions that would curtail Travancore’s sovereignty.When diplomacy turned to threat, Imhoff warned of war.

Varma in turn shot back

*“I have been thinking about invading Europe someday.”*

A line delivered not in jest, but in calculated bravado — a rhetorical cannonball that shattered colonial arrogance.It wasn’t about literal conquest — it was a symbolic inversion of power. Varma was declaring that Travancore would no longer be dictated to, not by Europeans, not by feudal lords, not by history.

In late 1739, the Dutch East India Company, stung by Marthanda Varma’s defiance, cast diplomacy aside and declared war. Their goal was clear: to crush Travancore’s rising power and reclaim control over the spice trade.

A seasoned commander, Captain Hackert led Dutch forces in a swift and aggressive campaign. They forced the Travancore army to retreat from Kollam, a key port and strategic stronghold.Emboldened, the Dutch advanced to Attingal and Varkala, threatening the very heart of Varma’s domain.

But Marthanda Varma was no ordinary monarch — he was a tactician who understood that wars are won not just on battlefields, but in the minds of men.

In 1740, the Travancore army regrouped, fortified by discipline, morale, and a growing sense of purpose.They launched precision attacks on Dutch factories, targeting supply lines and trade outposts.

Dutch goods were seized, their warehouses emptied — a symbolic and economic blow. This wasn’t just retaliation — it was economic warfare, striking at the very foundation of Dutch power: commerce.

On November 26, 1740, Dutch forces landed at Colachel, a strategic port in what is now Kanyakumari district. The Dutch had fortified the area earlier, and now returned with three sloops and two large warships, unleashing a barrage on the coast.Nearby outposts — Midalam, Kadiapattinam, Eraniel — were bombarded, their defenses shaken.

Dutch commander Gollenesse declared a complete blockade of the Travancore coast around Colachel. The goal, isolate Travancore, cut off reinforcements, and starve the kingdom into submission.

In January 1741, the Dutch ship *Maarseveen* sailed further south, patrolling the waters between Thengapattanam and Colachel.But here, history took an unexpected turn ,local fishermen, the Mukkuvars, rose in defiance. Using their intimate knowledge of the coast and sheer courage, they halted the Dutch advance, buying precious time for Travancore’s counterattack.

As the Dutch tightened their grip on Colachel, fate dealt them a cruel hand. The Dutch East India Company, overstretched and embroiled in conflicts in Java and Batavia, found itself unable to send reinforcements. Their blockade, though fierce, was now vulnerable ,and Marthanda Varma saw it.

Marthanda Varma, ever the tactician, recognized the Dutch distraction as a fleeting window.He rushed south, mobilizing his forces with urgency and precision. At the same time, he entrusted his Dewan, Ramayyan Dalawa, with a critical mission: march to Colachel, confront the Dutch, and reclaim the coast.

Ramayyan Dalawa, a brilliant administrator and strategist, led the Travancore army with discipline and resolve.His forces moved swiftly, navigating terrain and morale with equal mastery.The goal was clear: break the blockade, crush the Dutch garrison, and reclaim Colachel.

As the Dutch garrison braced for reinforcements that would never come, Ramayyan Dalawa arrived like a storm from the north — not just with soldiers, but with a vision of total encirclement.

Ramayyan reached Kalkulam, commanding a formidable force. Infantry, cavalry, and artillery, trained in hybrid tactics. A fleet of native boats, swift and maneuverable, perfect for coastal disruption. A full unit of Mukkuvars, now acting as spies and scouts, tracking Dutch movements with unmatched precision.

Ramayyan stationed his troops between Nagercoil and Eraniel, effectively cutting off land access to Colachel. Meanwhile, Travancore’s patrol boats sliced through the waves, severing Dutch supply lines from the sea.The Dutch garrison, once confident, now found itself trapped — surrounded by land, sea, and silence.

While his army encircled the enemy, Marthanda Varma turned inward ,to faith, to resolve, to destiny. At the Adikesava Perumal Temple in Thiruvattar, he prayed before the deity, seeking divine sanction for the battle ahead. In a moment of profound symbolism, he consecrated his sword ,transforming it from a weapon of war into a vessel of dharma.

Among the many forces that marched under Marthanda Varma’s banner, none were as iconic or decisive as the Travancore Nair Brigade, known locally as the Nair Pattalam.

Formed as an elite fighting unit, the Nair Pattalam was initially composed exclusively of Nairs, a community with deep martial traditions.Their training emphasized discipline, agility, and loyalty, making them the backbone of Travancore’s military campaigns.

The brigade’s first commander, Kumaraswamy Pillai, was a tactician and leader who shaped the unit into a formidable force.

The Nair Pattalam was the spearhead in battles, often leading charges and holding critical positions. They played a decisive role in the Travancore–Dutch War, especially during Colachel, where their coordination with Ramayyan Dalawa’s forces turned the tide.

Over time, the brigade opened its ranks to other communities, reflecting a broader social integration. After independence, in 1954, the Nair Pattalam was formally merged into the Indian Army, becoming the 9th and 16th Battalions of the Madras Regiment. This transition marked the continuation of Travancore’s martial legacy into modern India — a rare lineage that spans centuries.

On August 10, 1741, the winds off the Malabar coast carried more than salt and spice ,they carried the roar of revolution. At Colachel, the forces of Travancore, led by Marthanda Varma and Ramayyan Dalawa, clashed with the Dutch in a battle that would echo across centuries.

Travancore’s army, reinforced by Dalawa’s unit from Malabar, moved with precision and fury. Local fishermen, the Mukkuvars, joined the charge ,not as conscripts, but as patriots defending their coast.The Dutch, entrenched and confident, were caught off guard by the speed and coordination of the assault.

Travancore’s forces broke through Dutch ranks, overwhelming their defenses.By August 14, all Dutch fortifications in the region were captured. The Dutch were forced to retreat to Kochi, their dreams of West Coast dominance shattered.

Among the prisoners was Commander Eustachius De Lannoy, along with Donadi. Marthanda Varma, in a gesture of magnanimity and foresight, pardoned them. Rather than treating them as enemies, he enlisted their expertise to modernize the Travancore army, providing European-style training,fortification techniques, artillery and logistics reforms.

De Lannoy would go on to become the Valiya Kapithan (Senior Captain) of Travancore, serving loyally and transforming the kingdom’s military infrastructure.

The battle marked the first major defeat of a European power by an Indian kingdom. It ended Dutch expansionist ambitions on the West Coast.It catalyzed the birth of a modern Travancore army, blending indigenous valor with European precision.

After the thunderous victory at Colachel, Marthanda Varma’s vision of a unified, sovereign Travancore was nearly complete. But one final challenge remained,the Kingdom of Kochi, still backed by Dutch forces, still clinging to its forts and trade routes.

Led by Eustachius De Lannoy, now commander of the Travancore Army, the forces marched north. The battle of Ambalapuzha was decisive and swift, showcasing the modernized tactics De Lannoy had introduced, which included coordinated infantry and artillery,fort siege techniques,naval support from Travancore’s patrol boats.

Kochi’s forces, weakened and divided, fell under the coordinated assault.The Dutch, unable to recover from Colachel and stretched thin across Asia, suffered another crushing defeat.

De Lannoy’s transformation of the Travancore army laid the foundation for a military force that would withstand the storms of empire, long after Varma’s reign.

During the Third Anglo-Mysore War, Tipu Sultan, backed by French-trained forces, swept through Kerala with devastating speed. Malabar fell quickly,its fragmented principalities and unprepared militias were no match for Tipu’s disciplined army. But when he turned toward Travancore, he met a wall ,both literal and metaphorical.

The Nair Pattalam, now a seasoned force, held the lines with precision and courage. The Nedumkotta fortifications, built under De Lannoy’s guidance, slowed and frustrated Tipu’s advance. Travancore’s forces launched counterattacks, exploiting terrain and tactical discipline.Tipu, despite his military might, was forced to retreat,Travancore remained unconquered.

The Dutch defeat at Colachel in 1741 was more than a military collapse ,it was an economic disaster. For centuries, European powers had vied for control over Kerala’s pepper trade, the “black gold” that fueled fortunes from Amsterdam to Batavia. But after Colachel, the tide turned.

With Dutch forts captured and their naval blockade broken, Travancore gained full control over the pepper-producing regions. Varma’s annexation of Kollam, Attingal, and parts of Kochi ensured that key spice routes and ports were now under Travancore’s command. The kingdom began to negotiate trade on its own terms, leveraging its monopoly to strengthen its economy and diplomatic standing.

The Dutch, once dominant along the Malabar Coast, were reduced to minor commercial players. They continued to sell Indonesian sugar in India until 1795, but their political and military influence was effectively over. In 1795, the British East India Company absorbed Dutch holdings, marking the final exit of the Dutch from Indian affairs.

After the Battle of Colachel in 1741, King Marthanda Varma erected the Victory Pillar near Colachel beach to commemorate the defeat of the Dutch East India Company — a rare instance of an Asian power vanquishing a European naval force both on land and sea.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yrzj!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F72885d02-f69b-4fa6-b782-ac6574738aac_680x510.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!yrzj!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F72885d02-f69b-4fa6-b782-ac6574738aac_680x510.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The pillar is tar-black, capped with a conch, the royal insignia of Travancore,symbolizing divine protection and sovereignty. It’s tucked into a landscaped plot amid the town’s narrow streets, quietly asserting its historical weight. The site is now part of Tamil Nadu’s Kanyakumari district, but its legacy belongs to Travancore.

The parade ground at Pangode Military Station in Thiruvananthapuram is named after Colachel, and in 2023, a Colachel Victory Warrior Statue was unveiled there.

The statue honors the Travancore soldiers and sailors who fought valiantly under Marthanda Varma. The Madras Regiment, which traces its lineage to these forces, commemorates the battle annually with wreath-laying ceremonies and public displays.

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1
